diagrams.title: Task Creation and Execution Flow in OpenMP
diagrams.description: Shows the lifecycle of OpenMP tasks from creation to execution by worker threads
elements: Main thread at top creating tasks with #pragma omp task
Task queue (pending tasks pool) below main thread
Multiple worker threads (Thread 1, Thread 2, Thread 3, etc.) below the queue
Arrows showing tasks being dequeued and assigned to threads
Task execution boxes showing actual work being performed
Dashed arrow returning to queue showing thread availability after task completion
Key components: Task Generation Point, Task Pool, Thread Pool, Task Execution
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the dynamic nature of task-based parallelism - tasks are created by one thread but can be executed by any available thread from the pool. This illustrates work-stealing behavior and the decoupling of task creation from execution.

diagrams.title: Task Dependency Graph with depend Clause
diagrams.description: Visual representation of task dependencies using directed acyclic graph (DAG)
elements: Rectangular task nodes: Task A (read x), Task B (write x), Task C (read y), Task D (write y)
Dependency arrows with labels: depend(in) shown in blue, depend(out) shown in red, depend(inout) shown in purple
Arrow from Task A to Task B labeled 'depend(in: x)'
Arrow from Task C to Task D labeled 'depend(in: y)'
Independent tasks (no arrows between them) can execute in parallel
Timeline showing overlapping execution of independent tasks
Synchronization point showing all tasks complete before proceeding
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how to express data dependencies between tasks using the depend clause. This is crucial for correctness in producer-consumer patterns and ensures proper ordering of tasks that share data.

diagrams.title: Task Synchronization with taskwait
diagrams.description: Shows how taskwait directive synchronizes tasks within a parallel region
elements: Parallel region boundary (dashed rectangle)
Multiple tasks created inside parallel region: Task 1, Task 2, Task 3
#pragma omp taskwait barrier shown as vertical line
Code continuation point after taskwait
Arrows showing parent thread waiting at taskwait
Dashed arrows showing child tasks completing
Flow continues only after all tasks finish
Option to show #pragma omp taskwait depend on specific tasks
diagrams.purpose: Students understand explicit synchronization in tasking. They learn when and how to use taskwait to ensure child tasks complete before the parent proceeds, essential for passing results between tasks.

diagrams.title: Task Scheduling Visualization with untied vs tied
diagrams.description: Compares tied (default) and untied task scheduling behavior across threads
elements: Timeline axis (horizontal) showing time progression
Thread 1 and Thread 2 vertical lanes
Tied task example: Task X starts on Thread 1, interrupted, resumes on same Thread 1 (shown with pause arrows staying in Thread 1 lane)
Untied task example: Task Y starts on Thread 1, interrupted, resumes on Thread 2 (shown with task jumping between lanes)
Work-stealing illustration: Thread 2 steals pending task from Thread 1's queue
Key legend: Tied (solid color), Untied (pattern), Interrupted points
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the difference between tied and untied tasks and how this affects task migration between threads. This is important for understanding OpenMP runtime behavior and debugging task-related issues.

diagrams.title: Recursive Tasking: Divide and Conquer Pattern
diagrams.description: Shows how tasks can create child tasks for recursive algorithms like quicksort or tree traversal
elements: Root task at top (divide step)
Two child tasks below (conquer subproblems)
Further subdivision showing task tree structure
Base case tasks at bottom (no further subdivision)
Depth indicator showing recursion levels
Parallel execution zones showing independent branches running simultaneously
Synchronization at each level with implicit taskwait
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how tasking naturally expresses recursive divide-and-conquer algorithms. This pattern is essential for parallelizing tree traversals, quicksort, mergesort, and similar algorithms where workload is dynamically generated.

charts.title: Task vs Loop Parallelism: When to Use Each
charts.content:
Comparison table with columns: Criterion | Tasking | Loop Parallelism (parallel for)

Best for: Irregular/unknown iteration count | Regular/known iteration count

Workload: Dynamic, unpredictable at compile time | Static, known loop bounds

Scheduling: Runtime work-stealing | Static/dynamic at loop level

Examples: Tree traversal, graph algorithms, recursive functions | Matrix operations, array processing, numerical integration

Synchronization: Explicit taskwait/dependencies | Implicit barrier at end of parallel for

Data dependencies: Complex, requires depend clause | Simple, iteration-independent

Load balancing: Automatic via task queue | Depends on schedule type

Expressiveness: Higher, can express any parallelism | Lower, loop-centric only

charts.title: Decision Guide: Choosing Tasking in OpenMP
charts.content: Flowchart showing decision process: Start → Is workload in a loop with known iterations? → If YES: Can iterations run independently? → If YES: Use parallel for | If NO: Use tasks with depend clause | If NO (no known iterations): Is this recursive/divide-and-conquer? → If YES: Use recursive tasking | If NO: Is workload irregular/unstructured? → If YES: Use tasking | If NO: Consider other patterns → End → Recommendation based on path taken

charts.title: OpenMP Task Directives and Clauses Quick Reference
charts.content:
Table with columns: Directive/Clause | Syntax | Purpose

#pragma omp task | #pragma omp task [clause-list] | Create a task

#pragma omp taskwait | #pragma omp taskwait | Wait for child tasks

depend(in) | depend(in: var-list) | Task reads specified variables

depend(out) | depend(out: var-list) | Task writes specified variables

depend(inout) | depend(inout: var-list) | Task reads and writes variables

if | if(task-condition) | Conditionally create task

final | final(task-condition) | Create final task (no further splitting)

untied | untied | Allow task to execute on any thread

priority | priority(priority-value) | Set task scheduling priority

mergeable | mergeable | Allow task to be merged with parent

defaultpriority | defaultpriority(priority-value) | Set default task priority

charts.title: Task Synchronization Methods Comparison
charts.content:
Table comparing synchronization approaches: Method | When to Use | Behavior

Implicit barrier (end of parallel region) | All tasks must complete before region ends | All tasks in region wait

taskwait | Need to wait for child tasks created in current task | Current task suspends until children complete

taskgroup | Wait for group of tasks with taskgroup directive | Waits for all tasks in taskgroup

Dependencies (depend clause) | Ensure ordered execution based on data | Runtime ensures proper ordering

flush | Ensure memory visibility | Synchronizes memory across threads

single | Only one thread executes block | Implicit synchronization

branches: Task Creation: #pragma omp task, task generation point, implicit task
Task Execution: thread pool, work-stealing, task scheduling, tied vs untied
Task Dependencies: depend(in), depend(out), depend(inout), DAG-based ordering
Task Synchronization: taskwait, taskgroup, implicit barriers
Task Control Clauses: if, final, mergeable, priority, defaultpriority
Task Attributes: task, taskloop, taskloop simd
Performance Considerations: task granularity, dependency overhead, false sharing
Comparison: task vs parallel for, when to use each approach
Real Applications: tree traversal, graph algorithms, recursive sorting, irregular meshes
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview of OpenMP tasking concept showing relationships between task creation, execution, synchronization, and practical use cases. Students can see how all components connect and when to apply different tasking features.

branches: depend(in): Read-only access, consumer task, no output modifications
depend(out): Write-only access, producer task, creates/defines new values
depend(inout): Read-write access, both consumes and produces
Dependency Graph: DAG representation, topological ordering, parallel execution of independent tasks
Execution Model: Runtime analyzes dependencies, schedules tasks respecting order, enables maximum parallelism
Common Pitfalls: Missing dependencies (race conditions), unnecessary dependencies (reduced parallelism), circular dependencies (not allowed)
Examples: Matrix chain multiplication,LU decomposition, parallel graph algorithms
mindMaps.purpose: Focuses specifically on how to express and handle data dependencies in task-based parallelism, which is critical for correct and efficient parallel programs.
